info.name: GateGuard API — CS432 IIT Gandhinagar
info.description:
GateGuard API collection for CS432 Databases, Assignment 2 Module B, IIT Gandhinagar.

Stack: Node.js 18, Express 4, PostgreSQL 14, JWT auth.

How to get started:
1. Set BASE_URL to http://localhost:5000 in collection variables.
2. Run Health Check to confirm the server is running.
3. Run Login — the test script saves ACCESS_TOKEN and REFRESH_TOKEN automatically.
4. Every other request picks up the token from collection variables.

Collection variables:
BASE_URL — the server address, default http://localhost:5000
ACCESS_TOKEN — set by Login and Refresh Token
REFRESH_TOKEN — set by Login and Refresh Token
GUARD_TOKEN — set by Login as Guard (in RBAC Tests folder)
MEMBER_ID — set by Create Member and Get Member
VEHICLE_ID — set by Create Vehicle
GATE_ID — set by Get Gate
VISIT_ID — set by Record Person Entry
VV_VISIT_ID — set by Record Vehicle Entry
USER_ID — set by Login
RESET_TOKEN — set by Forgot Password (needed for Verify OTP and Reset Password)

RBAC summary:
Guard: read everything, record entries and exits.
Admin: all of the above plus create/update members, vehicles, gates, manage users, view audit logs.
SuperAdmin: everything including deletes and direct user creation.

Every POST, PUT, DELETE goes to both logs/audit.log (Winston) and the AuditLog PostgreSQL table with OldValue and NewValue as JSONB. If you write directly to the DB without going through the API, there will be no audit record — that's how you tell it was unauthorised.

All responses use the same shape:
success true: { success: true, data: {...}, pagination: {...} }
error: { success: false, error: { message: '...', fields: {} } }

Folder order matches a typical testing workflow — Health first, Auth second (get your token), then Members/Vehicles/Gates to set up data, then Visits to use it, then Dashboard/Audit to inspect results, and finally RBAC Tests to verify access control.

item.name: Health
item.description: Health check and welcome. No auth needed. Run these first.

request.description: Root endpoint. Returns the API name and version. No auth needed.
Basic health check. Returns healthy and the current server timestamp. Hit this first to make sure the server is up before running anything else.

item.name: Auth
item.description: Login, logout, token refresh, password reset via OTP, and user registration. Tokens are saved automatically by test scripts.

request.description:
Logs in with username and password. Returns a short-lived access token (15 min) and a long-lived refresh token (7 days). The test script saves both to collection variables so you don't have to copy-paste them.

After 5 wrong passwords the account gets locked for 30 min. When that happens the response comes back 423 with a lockedUntil timestamp — the frontend uses that to show a countdown.

All three demo accounts use the same password: admin123.

Gets a fresh access token without logging in again. The old refresh token is immediately invalidated and a new one is returned — that way even if someone sniffs the refresh token, replaying it after rotation fails.

The test script overwrites both ACCESS_TOKEN and REFRESH_TOKEN automatically.
Returns whoever is currently logged in based on the JWT. The middleware re-queries the DB on every call so stale JWT claims (like a role change) get caught here rather than on the next mutation.

Logs out by writing the access token to the tokenblacklist table and revoking the refresh token. Even though the JWT is technically valid for up to 15 min, the blacklist check inside authenticate() will reject it immediately.

Send the refresh token in the body so it gets cleaned up server-side too.

Kicks off the OTP password reset flow. Always returns 200 regardless of whether the email exists — that's intentional, prevents someone from scanning which emails are registered.

If the email is found, a 6-digit OTP and a resetToken are generated and stored with a 15-minute expiry. The OTP goes to the user's email via Nodemailer. The resetToken comes back in the response — hold onto it for the next two steps.

During testing you can read the OTP directly from the DB:
SELECT otp FROM passwordresettoken ORDER BY createdat DESC LIMIT 1;
Checks that the resetToken and OTP are a valid pair. Four things have to be true: the token exists, hasn't been used, hasn't expired, and the 6 digits match. Any of those fails and you get 400.

Finishes the password reset. Hashes the new password with bcrypt (12 rounds), marks the OTP as used so it can't be replayed, and revokes every existing refresh token for that user — forces a logout on all devices. A confirmation email goes out via Nodemailer.

The server re-validates the OTP here rather than trusting that you already called verify-otp.

Changes the password for the currently logged-in user. You have to supply the current password — no bypassing it. On success, all other active sessions are killed by revoking their refresh tokens. Also clears the mustChangePassword flag if it was set (e.g. on first login).
Returns the last 10 login attempts for the current user — IP address, browser/agent string, timestamp, and whether it succeeded or not.
SuperAdmin-only endpoint to create a user account directly. Password needs at least 8 chars with one uppercase, one lowercase, and one number. Role IDs: 1 = SuperAdmin, 2 = Admin, 3 = Guard.

item.name: Members
item.description: CRUD on campus members. Read is open to all authenticated users. Create and update need Admin or above. Delete is SuperAdmin only.

request.description:
Paginated member list with type info joined in. Supports search by name or email (case-insensitive ILIKE), filter by typeId, and sorting.

Note on search performance: the idx_member_name_lower expression index helps with prefix searches (e.g. 'raj%') but can't help with a leading wildcard ('%raj%'). That limitation is documented in the assignment report.
All rows from the membertype table — Faculty, Student, Staff, Visitor, etc. Useful for populating dropdowns in the UI.
Single member record including their type name, last 5 visits, and registered vehicles. Set MEMBER_ID in the collection variables or edit the URL directly.

Creates a member record. Admin or SuperAdmin only.

Required: name, email (must be unique), contactNumber, typeId.
Optional: age (1-120), department.

Every successful create writes to both logs/audit.log and the AuditLog table with the full new record in NewValue JSONB.

Partial update — only include the fields you actually want to change. Joi rejects the request if you send an empty object. The old values get captured before the UPDATE and stored in AuditLog.OldValue so you can see exactly what changed.

Hard delete. SuperAdmin only.

PersonVisit rows referencing this member have personid set to NULL (ON DELETE SET NULL). Vehicles this member owns will block the delete if ON DELETE RESTRICT is in place — delete the vehicles first. The deleted record goes into AuditLog.OldValue.

item.name: Vehicles
item.description: CRUD on registered vehicles. Same access rules as Members.

request.description: Paginated list of vehicles with owner and type details. Search by license plate is case-insensitive.
All rows from the vehicletype table. Used for dropdowns.
Single vehicle with owner member details and vehicle type.

Creates a vehicle linked to a member. Admin or SuperAdmin only.

registrationNumber gets uppercased automatically and must be unique. ownerId must point to an existing member.
Partial update. At least one field required. Admin or SuperAdmin only.
Hard delete. SuperAdmin only. Blocked if active vehicle visits exist.

item.name: Gates
item.description: Manage campus gates and their occupancy counts.

request.description: All gates with occupancy counts joined in from gateoccupancy table.
Single gate with its occupancy row.
Creates a campus gate. SuperAdmin only. Status must be Active or Inactive.
Partial update on gate info. Admin or SuperAdmin only.
Hard delete. SuperAdmin only. Will fail with FK error if any visit record references this gate — visits must be cleaned up first.
Current occupancy count for all gates.

Updates the people count at a gate. Admin or SuperAdmin only.

occupancyCount is required and can't go below 0. capacityLimit and emergencyOverride are optional. incidentNote gets stored in the audit record.

item.name: Person Visits
item.description: Record people coming in and going out. Guards can do this.

query.description: true = active visits only (exittime IS NULL)

request.description:
Paginated person visits with member and gate names joined in. Use active=true to get only visits where exittime is NULL (the partial index on that column brought the query from 8ms to 6ms on the benchmark dataset).

The entrytime index (idx_person_visit_entry_time) is what made the sorted listing drop from 15ms to 0.1ms — it pre-sorts the rows so the ORDER BY doesn't need an in-memory sort.
Single visit record with member, entry gate, exit gate, and vehicle info.

Records when someone walks in. Any authenticated user can do this, including Guard.

memberId and entryGateId are required. vehicleId is optional — include it if the person drove in.

Sets entrytime to NOW() and leaves exittime NULL.

Records when someone leaves. Any authenticated user, including Guard.

Sets exittime = NOW() and exitgateid. The DB enforces exittime >= entrytime via CHECK constraint.
Hard delete. SuperAdmin only.

item.name: Vehicle Visits
item.description: Record vehicles coming in and going out. Guards can do this.

query.description: true = active visits only (exittime IS NULL)

request.description: Paginated vehicle visits. Same index story as person visits — the partial index on exittime IS NULL brought active-only queries from 6ms to 5ms.
Single vehicle visit with vehicle, owner, and gate details.
Records when a vehicle drives in. Any authenticated user including Guard.
Records when a vehicle leaves. Any authenticated user including Guard.
Hard delete. SuperAdmin only.

item.name: Dashboard
item.description: Aggregate stats, recent events, and daily trends for the dashboard UI.

request.description: Summary numbers for the dashboard: total members, total vehicles, active person visits, active vehicle visits, and gate occupancy. All in one call.
Last 10 events across both person and vehicle visits, merged and sorted by most recent. Used for the activity feed on the dashboard.
Daily visit counts for the past N days. Defaults to 7. Powers the area chart on the dashboard.

item.name: Users
item.description: User account management with an approval workflow for Admin-created accounts.

request.description: All user accounts with role info. Admin or SuperAdmin only. Password hash is never included in any response from this API.
Single user account with role. Admin or SuperAdmin only.

Creates a user. Behaviour depends on who's calling:
SuperAdmin creates the account immediately and it's active straight away.
Admin creates a pending request that SuperAdmin has to approve.

Password needs 8+ chars, at least one uppercase, lowercase, and number. Role IDs: 1 = SuperAdmin, 2 = Admin, 3 = Guard.
Lists user creation requests from Admins that are waiting on SuperAdmin approval. SuperAdmin only.
Approves the pending request and creates the user account. SuperAdmin only.
Rejects the pending request. SuperAdmin only.

Updates a user. What you can change depends on your role:
SuperAdmin can change username, password, and roleId for anyone.
Admin and Guard can only change their own password.

At least one field must be in the body.
Hard delete. SuperAdmin only. The service blocks you from deleting your own account.

item.name: Roles
item.description: Read-only. Lists the three roles: Guard, Admin, SuperAdmin.

request.description: All roles in the role table (Guard, Admin, SuperAdmin). Read-only — roles can't be created or deleted through the API.
Single role by ID.

item.name: Member Types
item.description: Read-only reference data for member type dropdowns.

request.description: All rows from the membertype table — Faculty, Student, Staff, Visitor, etc. Useful for populating dropdowns in the UI.
Single member type by ID.

item.name: Vehicle Types
item.description: Read-only reference data for vehicle type dropdowns.

request.description: All rows from the vehicletype table. Used for dropdowns.
Single vehicle type by ID.

item.name: Audit Logs
item.description: Read-only audit trail. Admin or SuperAdmin only. Every mutation in the system ends up here.

query.description: Filter by table name e.g. Member

request.description:
Paginated audit log. Admin or SuperAdmin only. These records can never be modified through the API — no POST, PUT, or DELETE on this endpoint.

Filter by action (CREATE/READ/UPDATE/DELETE), userId, tableName, or date range. Each record has OldValue and NewValue as JSONB — you can query the exact diff with standard SQL directly in psql.

The composite index on (userid, action, createdat DESC) means filtered+sorted queries don't need a full table scan.
Single audit log record. Useful for inspecting the exact OldValue and NewValue JSONB for a specific mutation.

item.name: RBAC Tests
item.description: Concrete tests that prove role enforcement is working. Runs as Guard and checks which requests get through and which get blocked.

request.description: Logs in as the guard demo account. The test script saves the token to GUARD_TOKEN so the next few requests can use it to demonstrate RBAC.

Guard tries to delete a member. Should get 403.

The authorize(ROLES.SUPERADMIN) middleware runs before any controller code and rejects the request as soon as it sees the Guard role. The test asserts the 403 so you can run it and confirm RBAC is wired correctly.

response.name: 403 Forbidden — Guard role insufficient

request.description: Guard tries to list users. Should get 403. Admin+ is required for this endpoint. Same middleware check, different route.

exec: pm.test('Guard blocked from users list with 403', () => pm.response.to.have.status(403));

request.description:
Guard records a person entry. Should succeed with 201.

This is the positive RBAC test — Guard has exactly the permissions needed for day-to-day gate duty, nothing more.

exec: pm.test('Guard allowed to record entry — status 201', () => pm.response.to.have.status(201));

request.description:
Sends a clearly invalid token. Should get 401.

The authenticate() middleware calls jwt.verify() which throws on an expired or malformed token, and the error handler returns 401 before any route logic runs.

response.name: 401 Token expired or revoked
